So a few years ago, a friend of mine was staying over at my house in Cambridge on route to a conference in a nearby state.
And this guy, I'll call him Bob, was a close friend of mine. I'd known him for years and years.
We talked regularly, we went on hiking trips together, we were pretty close.
So he's on route to this conference. He stayed over at my house the night before.
And the plan was to get up early the next morning and drive to the conference.
So we hung out the night before and chatted. And the next morning, he's sleeping in the next room over from mine.
And early in the morning, I hear some shuffling, I think yep, okay Bob is packing to leave and thank God I need to get up. I'm only dimly awake.
And so I'm not paying them a lot of attention.
Shuffling, shuffle, shuffle in the background.
And then I hear a crash. And I think what the hell is that?
And I get up and I go into the next room and Bob is lying on the floor, not moving.
I say Bob, and there's no answer. And then I shut Bob, and there's no answer.
And then I dialed 911. While we were sitting there waiting for the ambulance to arrive, Bob starts to wake up.
And he's very woozy, but he's alive, he's making a little bit of sense.
And he can't figure out what's going on and neither can I.
And so we're talking and chatting, he's making a little more sense, but we still don't know what's happening.
So then the ambulance arrives incredibly fast.
So like three minutes, boom, there's three EMTs rushing in the front door, rushing up to the room where Bob was.
And they take all his vitals, and they can't find anything wrong.
And so they're really casual. I guess they confront stuff like this all the time.
I don't. Bob doesn't. But they're very calm about it. And they're saying, well, you can take into the hospital or not.
And I was like, I think we need to know what just happened, even though we seem okay.
We kind of need to know what this is all about. Don't you think?
You can take them to the ER. And I said, well, do we need to waste ambulance resources?
Or do you think it's safe if I drive in myself since there's a hospital not far away?
I think you can drive in yourself.
So I drive Bob to the Mount Auburn Hospital ER, which is like a son of a mile from my house.
And we do the usual ER thing, which is mostly waiting and waiting and waiting.
But various docs come by and they take all these tests and they take all these history, questions, and it goes on and on.
Basically they're just not finding anything.
So after about an hour or two of this, they're still doing tests. They don't want to quite let them go yet because they don't know what happened.
Everybody's calm about it. I figure, okay, fine. I got work to do.

And so I go into work.
But just before I go into work, I thought flashes through my mind.
And I say to the ER doc, you know, you should check Bob's brain.
And the reason that thought flash through my mind is that actually I had been worrying about Bob for a number of years.

I hadn't, it was kind of too horrifying to thought for me to really allow myself to realize I was worried about Bob's brain.

And on the one hand, this just didn't make any sense because he was fine in every other way.

I just had this sinking feeling of dread and a pit of my stomach.

It was like, no, you turn right and we gave the directions.
Another time, a friend of mine was driving with Bob's hometown.
And noticed that Bob didn't seem to know how to get to the grocery store.
In his hometown, where he lived for a really long time, and a trip he'd made hundreds of times.
Another time, I was at a conference in Germany.
And I saw, you know, there are these arrays of posters of people presenting usually pretty dry scientific things.

I saw that, oh my God, Bob wasn't that old.
I know Alzheimer's can very rarely strike early.
I just, I didn't want to think about it, but it was like rattling around in the back of my consciousness.

Right next to the Parahit Picampal Place area, which I discovered, and a nearby related region called British Blinguocortex, of course, and how the hell could I not have known?

In that earlier work, it had been nearly 20 years ago. I had a postdoc named Russell Epstein, and Russell was a computer vision guy, and he wanted to understand how we see by writing code to duplicate the algorithms that he thought.

I said, okay, Russell. We'll just scan people, looking at pictures of scenes and looking at other kinds of pictures.
